content.text: MDM-to-RAG index sync pipelines bridge the authoritative golden-record store and the vector index that powers retrieval-augmented generation. When a product description is corrected, a customer entity is merged, or a location record is retired, those changes must propagate to the embedding layer within a bounded latency window — otherwise the AI assistant answers with stale or deleted facts.

This pipeline is an event-driven integration: MDM writes trigger change-data-capture (CDC) events that flow through a message bus to an embedding worker, which upserts or deletes vectors in the RAG index. A separate consistency checker reconciles index state against the MDM golden-record store on a scheduled basis.

A RAG system is only as trustworthy as the freshness of its index. Stale embeddings introduce factual drift — the model confidently repeats information that the enterprise has already superseded. In high-stakes domains (regulatory filings, product specifications, patient records) this drift carries legal and safety risk.
Product master changes (price, specifications, discontinuation) reach customers via chat before stale descriptions cause mis-selling.
Customer entity merges update the context the model uses for personalisation, preventing cross-customer data bleed.
Compliance-driven deletions (right-to-erasure, GDPR Article 17) are reflected in the vector index within the required 30-day window.
Governance auditors can demonstrate that the RAG index reflects the current, certified golden record rather than an arbitrary cache.
CDC events provide sub-minute detection of MDM changes, enabling P1 content to be re-indexed within minutes.
Only records with changed RAG-content fields (name, description, attributes) incur embedding cost; metadata-only changes use cheaper metadata-update calls.
The log-sequence-number (LSN) from the CDC stream gives a total ordering of changes — enabling replay and point-in-time reconstruction.
MDM and the vector store evolve independently; the event bus is the integration contract.
Retry queues and dead-letter topics ensure no change event is silently dropped.
Full corpus re-embedding during MDM migrations is expensive. Budget for incremental-only pipelines in steady state, with periodic full reconciliation.
Event-driven sync introduces a lag between MDM update and vector index update. Latency depends on Kafka consumer lag, embedding throughput, and retry backoff.
You must operate CDC connectors, a message bus, embedding workers, and a consistency checker — four additional infrastructure components.
The chunking logic encodes assumptions about MDM schema. MDM schema changes require a coordinated pipeline update.

Debezium (or an equivalent log-mining connector) tails the MDM database transaction log and publishes row-level change events. Each event carries the before/after record images, a list of changed column names, and the log-sequence-number (LSN) that gives a total order across all changes.

A router on the Kafka consumer side inspects the entity type and changed fields to assign a priority and decide whether re-embedding is needed. DELETE and merge operations receive priority-1 (high) treatment. Attribute changes that do not affect text content bypass the embedding worker entirely.

The worker fetches the current golden record from MDM (not from the event payload, to avoid embedding a stale before-image), chunks it into overlapping windows, calls the embedding model, and upserts all resulting vectors. Old chunks for the same entity are replaced atomically.

A nightly job scans the MDM golden-record store and computes expected content hashes. It compares these against the content_hash metadata stored in the vector index and surfaces any divergence as an alert. Gaps are re-queued for embedding.

attrs.alt: MDM to RAG Index Sync Pipeline: Change Data Capture to Event Bus to Embedding Worker to Vector Index

content.text: The pipeline has five stages: MDM golden-record store → CDC connector (Debezium) → Event Bus (Kafka) → Embedding Worker + Consistency Checker → Vector Index. The Consistency Checker runs on a nightly schedule and can re-queue events directly.
Your RAG corpus is built from MDM golden records and the golden-record store receives frequent updates (more than 100 per day).
Regulatory or contractual SLAs require the RAG index to reflect MDM changes within a bounded window (e.g. 4 hours for P1, 24 hours for P2).
You need an audit trail proving that deleted PII is also removed from vector storage.
MDM and the vector database are operated by different teams and must be decoupled via a message bus.
Start with the data model and event schema for change events, then wire the CDC connector, then build the worker logic:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional
import hashlib, json

@dataclass
class MDMChangeEvent:
    entity_id: str
    entity_type: str          # 'product', 'customer', 'location'
    operation: str            # 'INSERT' | 'UPDATE' | 'DELETE'
    before: Optional[dict]    # None for INSERT
    after: Optional[dict]     # None for DELETE
    changed_fields: list[str]
    source_table: str
    lsn: str                  # log sequence number for ordering
    occurred_at: datetime

    def affects_rag_content(self) -> bool:
        # Return True if the change requires re-embedding
        rag_fields = {'name', 'description', 'category', 'attributes',
                      'canonical_name', 'aliases', 'summary'}
        return bool(rag_fields & set(self.changed_fields))

    def to_kafka_payload(self) -> dict:
        return {
            'entity_id': self.entity_id,
            'entity_type': self.entity_type,
            'operation': self.operation,
            'changed_fields': self.changed_fields,
            'lsn': self.lsn,
            'occurred_at': self.occurred_at.isoformat(),
        }

@dataclass
class IndexSyncJob:
    event: MDMChangeEvent
    embedding_model: str = 'text-embedding-3-large'
    priority: int = 1          # 1=high (DELETE/merge), 2=medium, 3=low
    max_retries: int = 3
    attempts: int = 0
    status: str = 'pending'    # pending | running | done | failed

    def should_delete_vector(self) -> bool:
        return self.event.operation == 'DELETE'

    def chunk_id(self, chunk_index: int) -> str:
        raw = f'{self.event.entity_id}:{chunk_index}'
        return hashlib.sha256(raw.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
The worker class processes each job type and calls the vector client API:
Pipeline configuration in YAML drives Debezium, routing rules, retry policy, and nightly reconciliation schedule:

# mdm-sync-pipeline.yaml
pipeline:
  name: mdm-rag-sync
  source:
    type: debezium-postgres
    topic_prefix: mdm
    tables:
      - golden_records
      - entity_attributes
    lsn_tracking: true

  routing:
    - entity_type: product
      priority: high
      embedding_model: text-embedding-3-large
    - entity_type: customer
      priority: medium
      pii_scrub: true
    - entity_type: location
      priority: low

  consistency:
    reconciliation_schedule: '0 2 * * *'   # nightly full scan
    max_lag_seconds: 300
    alert_channel: '#mdm-sync-alerts'

  retry:
    max_attempts: 3
    backoff_seconds: [5, 30, 120]
    dead_letter_topic: mdm.sync.dlq

A product steward corrects a specification error on product SKU-7821: the material field changes from 'Nylon' to 'Recycled Polyester'. The MDM database UPDATE triggers a Debezium event with changed_fields=['material', 'description']. The router assigns priority-2. The embedding worker fetches the current golden record, re-chunks the concatenated name + description + attributes text, and upserts 3 new vectors with the updated content_hash. Total latency from MDM commit to vector index update: 47 seconds.

A European retailer runs a 12-million-product MDM store. Their initial approach was nightly batch re-indexing, which caused a 23-hour freshness lag. After implementing CDC-driven sync with Debezium and Kafka, steady-state lag fell below 5 minutes for P1 changes and below 2 hours for P3. Embedding cost dropped 80 percent because only the 0.3 percent of products that changed each day are re-indexed. Compliance reported a successful GDPR deletion audit with zero vector residuals found for erased entities.
The before/after images in CDC events may be incomplete (e.g. TOAST columns in Postgres). Always fetch the current golden record from MDM before embedding.
Without comparing content hashes, a metadata-only MDM update (e.g. updated_by field) triggers an unnecessary and expensive re-embed.
Missing vector delete on entity merge:
When two golden records are merged, the surviving entity gets a new ID. The old vectors remain unless explicitly deleted, polluting retrieval results with duplicate content.
Transient embedding API errors or vector DB timeouts silently drop events unless a DLQ catches and retries them.
Parallel workers can process events out of order. Without LSN-based idempotency checks, an older after-image can overwrite a newer one.
Both the sync worker and the reconciliation job use it to skip unchanged records cheaply.
Use idempotent upserts keyed on chunk_id:
Deterministic chunk IDs derived from entity_id + chunk_index ensure that retrying an event is safe.
Route P1 events (DELETE, regulatory) to a dedicated consumer group with more workers; P3 events to a lower-priority group.
Event streams occasionally miss events (connector restart, compaction). A reconciliation job that scans MDM and compares hashes catches these silently.
Publish lag_seconds, events_processed, re_embed_count, and skip_count per entity_type to a metrics store. Alert when lag exceeds SLA thresholds.
Write integration tests that INSERT, UPDATE, then DELETE a golden record and assert that no vectors for the entity remain.

Embedding throughput is usually the bottleneck. A single embedding API call for a 200-token chunk takes approximately 100ms. At 1000 changed records per hour with 3 chunks each, you need about 3000 embedding calls per hour — within free-tier limits. At 100k changes per hour, you need horizontal embedding worker scaling and batch embedding APIs (up to 2048 inputs per call for OpenAI).
Group up to 2048 text chunks per API call. Sort by priority queue and flush at 500ms or 2048 items, whichever comes first.
For records with 50+ attributes, compute which attribute groups changed and only re-embed those chunks rather than the full record.
Kafka consumer groups scale horizontally. Add workers until embedding API rate limit is reached; then increase API quota or use a secondary provider.
Pinecone supports about 100 upsert operations per second per index per pod. Batch vector upserts (100 vectors per call) and scale pods as volume grows.
Open-source CDC connector for Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, Oracle. Publishes row-change events to Kafka topics.
Apache Kafka / AWS Kinesis / Google Pub/Sub:
Durable event buses with consumer group semantics and at-least-once delivery.
Vector databases with metadata filtering, upsert APIs, and delete-by-filter for bulk entity removal.
OpenAI Embeddings API / Cohere / Vertex AI:
Managed embedding endpoints with batch input support.
Open-source data integration platform with configurable source and destination connectors.
Stream processing for enrichment, deduplication, and LSN-ordered processing of change events.
No CDC support, medium change rate
For customer entity types, strip direct identifiers from the text before passing to the embedding model. Store entity_id in metadata only.
Kafka topics and vector store APIs should use TLS. Embedding API calls must use HTTPS.
Kafka's at-least-once delivery guarantee means the same event may be delivered twice. Idempotent upserts keyed on chunk_id prevent duplicate vectors.
Alert when the dead-letter queue depth exceeds 100 events. Stale DLQ events represent index drift.
Only the sync worker should have write access to the vector index. MDM should have read-only access for the embedding worker's record fetch.

Think of the MDM store as a library catalogue and the vector index as the physical shelves. When a librarian updates the catalogue (MDM), a re-shelving team (CDC + embedding worker) must move or re-label the relevant books on the shelves (vector index). The re-shelving team does not re-shelve every book every night — only the ones whose catalogue entry changed. A periodic shelf audit (reconciliation job) catches any books that were moved without updating the catalogue entry.
CDC-driven sync achieves minutes-level freshness at a fraction of the cost of nightly full re-indexing.
Fetch the current golden record from MDM at embedding time, not from the event payload, to avoid stale-image bugs.
Use content hashes to skip re-embedding when only non-content fields change.
Mandatory: delete vectors when golden records are merged or erased.
A nightly reconciliation job is the safety net for events the stream misses.
Store LSN in job metadata to enforce ordering and enable replay.
